articles.title: The Workdeck article
articles.notes: Source: substack-articles-and-experiments.md line 1. Workdeck = portmanteau of workbook + deck: hand someone a Claude repo that both tells them what they need to know AND walks them through a workflow to do it. Productization is unsolved and is the main thing Allen wants to crack; the article publicizes the idea and ships an example project. Ease is low because productization is open and an example build is needed.

articles.title: I run my entire todo list as a git repo Claude manages
articles.notes: Source: parked-todos repo. Show CLAUDE.md as the brain, the INGEST.md -> PROCESSED.md loop, /sync and /wrap skills. The hook: my todos talk back and act on themselves between sessions. Takeaway reader copies: the CLAUDE.md + INGEST pattern. Note: part of a potential workflow/consulting series.

Real artifact, real outcome. Takeaway reader copies: the classify-photos skill structure. Note: part of a potential workflow/consulting series.

articles.title: Scribe-to-ingest pipeline
articles.notes: Idea: take dictated/handwritten text files from Kindle Scribe and auto-parse them into project ingests. Lines starting with "Action:" get routed to the right project's INGEST.md. Ask which project if ambiguous. Could kick off new repo creation for big ideas. Send to a dedicated email (e.g. scribe@gmail.com) -> auto-parsed and pushed to GitHub. Cron or event-driven Claude runs against INGEST files; throttle to e.g. 5h after last run to control costs. Potential article: "Design and build an app from Kindle Scribe"

articles.title: What I learned reading everything I could from Chris Zukowski
articles.notes:
Source: User idea submission.

# Insights: How To Make Your First Game Blow Up On Steam
- The Steam algorithm doesn't care about the positivity or ratio of reviews (unless you're below Mixed). It strictly cares about gross revenue. If your game brings in money, Steam promotes it.
- 30,000 wishlists is the realistic threshold to aim for, giving you enough buffer to hit Steam’s algorithmic thresholds like "New and Trending". If you convert at normal rates from 30k wishlists, you’ll likely cross the threshold (~$8,000 in the first couple of days) for Valve to keep promoting you.
- 100,000 wishlists gives you the "pop-up" (when Steam is closed and reopened), but the jump from 30k to 100k has diminishing returns unless your game converts exceptionally well.
- Wishlist momentum acts as algorithmic "kindling". Having 100k wishlists is a blowtorch, 30k is a lighter. But if the game itself (the kindling) isn't engaging, it won't sustain a fire no matter the ignition source.
- Avoid building a "lukewarm" game. The biggest enemy on Steam is apathy. It's better to have a highly polarizing game where a niche actively throws money at it than a game everyone thinks is "okay" but no one buys.
- Do not use AI to generate art if you want your game to stand out. Generative AI leans toward the "average," contributing to the lukewarm, safe middle ground.
- Stop polling social media audiences on your art/design choices; averaging opinions makes the game average. You must trust your vision.
- Polish in marketing materials can mask a bad game, but only until launch. If the game isn't fun, Word of Mouth will kill it immediately regardless of your Wishlist count.
- Demos are the ultimate lie detector. Do not debut your demo during Next Fest; launch it months prior and get it tested by real players and streamers to eliminate game-breaking bugs.
- If you're going into Next Fest, hit the "email wishlisters" button within two weeks of launching your demo.

# Insights: How to Market a Game ft. Chris Zukowski and Larry Hyrb
- Real marketing starts with the genre. If you make a 2D Platformer, Puzzle game, or VR game, you are selling hamburger meat at a vegan co-op. The Steam audience fundamentally ignores certain genres.
- The most consistently successful Steam genres are "crafty, buildy, strategy, simulation". Sandbox mechanics, city builders, open world survival craft, horror, management, and roguelike deck builders thrive because they generate infinite playtime out of systemic mechanics rather than expensive, linear hand-crafted content.
- Do not rely on social media to build wishlists. Twitter/TikTok virality is a 0.1% unicorn outcome. True visibility comes from getting streamers/content creators to play your demo and participating in ongoing Steam Festivals.
- Your Steam page has two primary goals: instantly communicate what genre the game is (so players know if it "fits their shape") and prove high production quality.
- If your genre research reveals specific conventions (like crafting games always having a hammer in the capsule art), adhere to them. Subconscious signals are crucial for rapid player categorization.
- If you've run your game through streamers, a demo, and festivals and still struggle to hit 7,000 wishlists, the game likely won't sell. Avoid sunk cost fallacy: finish the game quickly, launch it, and start a new project.
- Steam is essentially a Horror grindhouse theater. Indie horror fans are extremely active buyers.

# Insights: The Indie Game Marketing Trick That's BEATING The Competition
- Wishlists do not get old. A wishlist from three years ago converts at the same rate as a wishlist from a month ago. However, if it took you three years to get 10,000 wishlists, that lack of velocity indicates low market excitement.
- Never use direct math to estimate your conversion rate (e.g. "I'll convert 10%"). Conversion is entirely dictated by the actual quality and fun-factor of your game upon release.
- A game's capsule art (thumbnail) is the first impression. Never attempt to draw it yourself unless you are a professional illustrator. Gamers spot amateur art instantly and will assume the game is equally amateur. Pay a professional.
- When making GIFs for your Steam page, avoid low framerates and ensure characters aren't clipping into objects. If a GIF is choppy, players assume the game runs at a terrible framerate.
- Demos for linear, narrative games should end on a massive cliffhanger. Just like movies, drop the player in the middle of the action with the best weapons/abilities, get them hooked, and immediately hit them with a "Wishlist Now" screen.
- Steam's Greenlight system never left; it just shifted from "upvotes" to "gross revenue." If you do not sell well in the first month (usually generating around 250 reviews), Steam will ignore your game permanently. There are virtually zero post-launch "resurrections".
- Paid ads (Facebook, Reddit) will not fix an uninteresting game. Zero multiplied by a large ad budget is still zero. Only deploy paid marketing if your free marketing (streamers, festivals) is already seeing explosive organic traction.
- Make smaller games first. In today's economy, aim to make a $10,000 game in 3 months rather than spending years chasing a million-dollar hit. Post to itch.io: if your free prototype organically hits 5,000 views, there's market validation to expand it.
